metadata.title: An Off Switch for Dangerous Knowledge.
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one earned accent per beat; mono for code/output only
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Norwegian one-word hello. Wagwan check: charsum('claude-liam-off-switch-gram') % 10 = 7 — not 0, so no Wagwan. Wagwan is Bear's alone; Liam never takes it.
metadata.description_blurb: Frontier models store dangerous knowledge alongside useful knowledge — and today's output filters don't remove it, they just hide it. Liam takes apart GRAM (Gradient-Routed Auxiliary Modules), a July 2026 paper from Anthropic and AE Studio that adds modular neurons per dual-use category, routes gradient flow during pretraining, and lets you delete the module to delete the capability — one training run, sixteen deployable configurations.
metadata.note: claude-liam claude-explainer. LAWS: B00=ClaudeComposerAsk cold open. IN-FOR-BEAR LAW: B00 first breath introduces Liam. Figures B01-B11 are conceptual animated diagrams (Manim/Remotion) — never screenshots. ILLUSTRATE LAW: Claude UI only at B00, B12 handoff, B13 outro. ASK→RESULT LAW applied. HANDOFF B12 second-to-last. OUTRO B13 restates title. Source: AE Studio × Anthropic, GRAM / modular pretraining paper, July 2026. On-screen numbers capped: 4 categories, 16 configurations, 3 test settings, 7 model sizes, 50M and 5B params.
metadata.caption_note: After Anthropic × AE Studio, GRAM / modular pretraining (2026) — appears once on GRAM diagram beats.

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'ClaudeComposerAsk916' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
END: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Liam, in for Bear. The move from guarding the exit door to routing dangerous knowledge into a deletable module is the part I want to understand mechanically. GRAM — Gradient-Routed Auxiliary Modules — is the paper.

props.segment: An Off Switch for Dangerous Knowledge
props.command: The move from guarding the exit door to routing dangerous knowledge into a deletable module is the part I want to understand mechanically. Take the virology module in GRAM. Can you (1) walk me through what happens to the general weights versus the virology module during a general-text pass and a virology-text pass, (2) explain why deleting that module removes the capability whereas refusal training left the room still full, and (3) tell me one way deletion could fail to fully remove the knowledge?

beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk cold open — Liam types the hook question

beats.narration_text: Here is the constraint. You want three things at once: lock dangerous knowledge surgically, still let trusted users reach it for legitimate purposes, and don't dent general performance on everything else. Three goals that must hold simultaneously.
beats.new_visual_element: Three-goal balance diagram — scalpel / key / gauge icons, terracotta when all three hold level

production_viz.label: Three Goals Must Hold
production_viz.mechanic: Three icons arranged in a triangle: a scalpel (surgical lock), a key (trusted access), a gauge (general performance intact). Lines connecting them. All three light up terracotta when balanced. Caption: 'After Anthropic × AE Studio, GRAM / modular pretraining (2026)'
production_viz.note: One terracotta moment: all three goals glowing simultaneously

beats.act: ACT 2 — THE BLUNT VERSION AND ITS COST
beats.narration_text: The obvious alternative: filter dual-use data out of pretraining. Virology text never enters the model. But this commits you to one model with one fixed set of capabilities. Want virology on for biosecurity researchers and virology off for public deployment? You train two complete models. At frontier scale, that is prohibitively expensive.
beats.new_visual_element: Filtering is blunt diagram — one filtered dataset, two parallel model stacks with cost tags

production_viz.label: One Setting, One Model — Expensive
production_viz.mechanic: Left column: a single dataset with a filter icon removing a 'virology' chunk. Arrow to ONE model box. Below it: two parallel stacks (Model A: virology-OFF, Model B: virology-ON) each with a dollar-sign cost chip. Red X on the two-stack arrangement. Ink on cream.
production_viz.note: No terracotta — this is the problem beat, keep it cool ink-on-cream to contrast with the GRAM reveal

beats.narration_text: GRAM — Gradient-Routed Auxiliary Modules — takes a different route. At every Transformer layer, you attach extra neurons. These extras are grouped into modules, one per dual-use category: virology, cybersecurity, nuclear physics, niche code. Four modules riding alongside the main model.
beats.new_visual_element: GRAM structure diagram — Transformer layer stack with colored extra neuron strips per module

production_viz.label: Four Modules, One Model
production_viz.mechanic: Vertical stack of four Transformer layer rectangles (main weights, warm ink). On the right side of each layer: four colored strips labeled 'virology', 'cybersecurity', 'nuclear', 'niche-code'. Caption bottom-right: 'After Anthropic × AE Studio, GRAM / modular pretraining (2026)'. Ink on cream.
production_viz.note: Terracotta on the module strips — one accent moment showing the new structure

beats.narration_text: Training has two kinds of passes. On general text, gradient flows through the whole network — everyone learns. On virology text, gradient routing kicks in: the general weights are frozen, only the virology module updates. Dangerous knowledge accumulates in its module and nowhere else.
beats.new_visual_element: Gradient routing diagram — two passes, general text lights all, virology text lights only module

production_viz.label: Knowledge Pools Into Its Module
production_viz.mechanic: Two side-by-side panels. Left: label 'general text', all layer weights glow softly with gradient arrows — whole network updates. Right: label 'virology text', general weights are gray and static, only the virology module strip glows terracotta with a 'gradient flows here' arrow. Between panels a split arrow labeled 'routing decision'.
production_viz.note: Terracotta only on the active virology module — the one accent moment

beats.narration_text: After training: delete the module. The capability disappears with it. The rest of the model is completely unchanged. Or leave the module in, behind an access key, for trusted deployment to biosecurity researchers. One base model, multiple configurations.
beats.new_visual_element: Off-switch diagram — module slides out, capability light goes dark, rest of model unchanged

production_viz.label: Delete the Module, Delete the Capability
production_viz.mechanic: Model stack diagram. Animation: the virology module strip slides out to the right and fades — a dotted outline remains labeled 'capability gone'. The main weights remain solid. OR branch below: same module strip with a key icon labeled 'trusted deployment — module retained'. Ink on cream, no accent on deletion (muted), small terracotta key for the trusted path.
production_viz.note: Terracotta on the key icon only — the one accent moment

beats.narration_text: Four categories. Each can be on or off. One training run produces sixteen deployable configurations — the full binary grid. That is the combinatorial win at the heart of GRAM.
beats.new_visual_element: 16-configuration grid — four toggle switches animating through 2^4 combos, 16 in terracotta

production_viz.label: One Run, Sixteen Configurations
production_viz.mechanic: Four toggle switches labeled virology / cybersecurity / nuclear / niche-code. Grid of 16 cells fills in as toggles cycle through all binary combinations. Large terracotta '16' appears when the grid completes. Caption: '2^4 = 16'. Ink on cream.
production_viz.note: Terracotta on the number 16 — the single accent moment for this beat

beats.narration_text: Three tests of rising realism. Test one: synthetic children's stories, topic-tagged. GRAM can forget any topic on demand. Each configuration performs approximately as well as a model trained from scratch on filtered data — without the cost of retraining from scratch.
beats.new_visual_element: Test 1 diagram — story corpus tagged by topic, GRAM config vs filtered model performance comparison

production_viz.label: GRAM ≈ From-Scratch Filtering
production_viz.mechanic: Left: a stack of story cards, each tagged with a colored topic label. Arrow to two bar groups: 'GRAM config' and 'filtered baseline' on each topic. Bars nearly equal height. Label: 'each config ≈ from-scratch filtered model'. Ink on cream.
production_viz.note: No strong accent — this is the baseline pass. Keep cool.

beats.narration_text: Test three: scale. Seven model sizes from fifty million to five billion parameters. At every size, GRAM matches filtering. And the gap between module-on and module-off grows as the models get larger — which means the mechanism gets stronger, not weaker, as you scale.
beats.new_visual_element: Scale line chart — 50M to 5B params, module-ON vs module-OFF gap widens, terracotta on gap

production_viz.label: Gap Grows With Scale
production_viz.mechanic: Line chart. X-axis: model size (7 points from 50M to 5B params). Two lines: 'module-ON' (stays high, ink) and 'module-OFF' (stays low, dashed ink). Shaded region between them grows as x increases. Terracotta fill on the widening gap region. X-axis labels: '50M' and '5B' only. Y-axis: capability score. No other numbers.
production_viz.note: Terracotta on the widening gap shading — one accent moment

beats.narration_text: Your prompt. Paste this into Claude: explain the difference between hiding knowledge and removing it. That is the core distinction GRAM is making — refusal hides, module deletion removes. The distinction matters because hiding leaves the room still full. Removing empties it. Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: Your Turn composer beat — viewer prompt typed in

props.segment: An Off Switch for Dangerous Knowledge
props.command: Explain the difference between hiding knowledge and removing it.

beats.narration_text: That's the short version. The full video also covers Guards the Door, Not the Room, Removed vs Suppressed and Three Caveats. One After Another. — watch An Off Switch for Dangerous Knowledge. for the whole story. The link is right below.

props.title: An Off Switch for Dangerous Knowledge.
props.subline: Research: AE Studio × Anthropic · Liam, in for Bear.

card.next: Let's recap with Claude. The goal is to lock dangerous knowledge surgically, still let trusted users reach it, and not dent general performance. Refusal training and classifiers guard the model's output while the underlying dangerous knowledge stays intact. GRAM attaches extra neuron modules per dual-use category at every Transformer layer, riding alongside the main model. Gradient routing sends dangerous-text updates only into that module, so deleting it removes the capability.
